behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: could be Integration because retries are part of a larger flow. Pass 2: the failure boundary here is the backoff calculation rule itself (attempt n -> delay), which is local logic with no required module handoff or I/O. REJECTION-GATE: rejected Integration/System because behavior text only specifies timing formula and cap, not transport, scheduler, or OS/network interaction.
behaviors.test_description: Test a pure backoff calculator (or retry-policy object) with consecutive failure counts and max-delay config; assert delays follow 2^n growth and clamp at configured maximum.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Unit if only status-code parsing. Pass 2: behavior explicitly combines response classification + retry wait computation, a module boundary (HTTP response handling -> retry policy). INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no real OS/network/subprocess required; deterministic fake responses are sufficient; no AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Wire client + retry module with a stub transport returning a 429-like response (and variants such as Retry-After). Assert retry path is entered and computed delay matches policy.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Integration. Pass 2: confirmed; failure appears when timer/backoff, request replay, and response propagation interact. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text does not require real HTTP/network conditions; in-process stub transport plus controlled clock can expose resend bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake clock + stub transport: first response is rate-limited, second succeeds. Assert same request payload is resent after delay and final resolved value is the success payload seen by caller.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed QuotaExhaustedError rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Unit for error mapping. Pass 2: Integration is safer minimum because max-retry counting and terminal error translation happen across retry loop + error factory/boundary. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no real OS/network/subprocess needed; no AI output involved.
behaviors.test_description: Configure max retries, stub transport to keep returning rate-limit errors, run call(), and assert rejection is QuotaExhaustedError (type and key fields), not raw/generic error.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: possibly System due to timing/loop risks. Pass 2: Integration is enough because loop termination can be proven with controlled clock and stubbed responses while exercising multiple local modules (retry controller + quota tracker + scheduler). REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because no behavior text requires real network/process/OS primitives.
behaviors.test_description: Run end-to-end in-process with fake clock and persistent rate-limit responses; assert bounded number of attempts, expected total scheduled waits, and final surfaced QuotaExhaustedError without hanging.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Integration. Pass 2: confirmed; real failure is shared-state coordination across concurrent callers and quota module, not a single function. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: text explicitly says "in the same process," which disqualifies need for System-level distributed/OS infra; no AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Fire multiple call() invocations concurrently against one client instance with deterministic stub transport; assert quota consumption is global (combined callers), not per-caller isolated.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1: Unit if only queue data structure. Pass 2: behavior is library-level dispatch order under waiting conditions, requiring interaction of queueing, retry/wait state, and sender module. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because order guarantees can be validated with in-process scheduler/clock control; no required real network/OS behavior in text.
behaviors.test_description: Queue several calls while quota-limited, advance controlled time to release sends, and assert transport observes FIFO dispatch matching original submission order.
